source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-07-08
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood SE), Priya Rao (Horizon Health), Marcus Lee (Horizon Infra), Samantha Ortiz (Product)

Summary:
Quick Optimize console walkthrough focused on quantization profiles (INT8 vs FP8), runbook for profile selection, and how to balance throughput/latency with clinical fidelity for Horizon Health's triage & summarization workloads. Customer wants an action plan and a short pilot to validate latency & QA metrics.

Topics covered:
- baseline profiling and what data we need
- INT8 sensitivity vs FP8 for token-level hallucination risk
- fallback rules in Optimize (when to switch to FP8 or model variant)
- console demo: profile editor, heatmaps, per-prompt quality delta
- security/hosting notes for PII in health prompts

Notes / Shared links:
- Sandbox link: https://app.redwood.ai/sandbox/horizon-health (Redwood will grant access)
- Optimize runbook: https://redwood.ai/docs/optimize/qprofiles (shared during call)
- Benchmark template (CSV) referenced: "Horizon-benchmark-template.csv" (Ava to attach)

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava: Hey everyone thanks for joining, we're excited to walk through the Optimize console and qprofiles today, we built this specifically to help teams like Horizon trim cost without losing clinical fidelity.
[00:18] Priya: Thanks Ava, quick context — we're running a triage/summarization stack, about 200k requests/day right now, peak latency target 600ms tail, and accuracy is basically patient safety first, cost second.
[00:35] Diego: Got it. Before we jump in, a couple clarifying Qs — are those requests mostly short prompts or longer context windows? That impacts quant choices.
[00:45] Marcus: Mostly multi-turn, average 512 tokens, some 1k for long case summaries. We also have strict data residency and SSO requirements.
[01:03] Ava: Perfect, we'll flag the VPC/dedicated notes later. Today we'll focus on INT8 vs FP8 tradeoffs; Diego will demo how the console highlights sensitivity per prompt and how to author fallback rules.
[01:22] Speaker 1 (background): mhm
[01:30] Diego: Pulling up the Optimize console — okay, here's the qprofile editor. On the left you see the profile selector: default, conservative, aggressive. Each row is a kernel/precision pair.
[01:45] Priya: Quick question — we've heard FP8 is newer and sometimes better at preserving subtlety, is that what you see?
[01:54] Diego: Yes, FP8 tends to keep distribution of logits closer to FP16, so for tasks sensitive to small logit differences — like negation or numeric accuracy — FP8 often beats INT8. But INT8 can be significantly faster and cheaper on certain GPUs.
[02:12] Ava: Also note that our runtime supports automatic prefix caching and KV cache reuse, which shifts the cost math — if you have a lot of repeated context, INT8 speedup compounds.
[02:27] Marcus: Are there models you recommend for INT8-first strategies?
[02:33] Diego: Short answer: depends on architecture. For some open LLMs we onboarded, INT8 with a conservative per-layer layout works well. We'll run a profile sweep on your actual model and prompts to recommend a default.
[02:45] Priya: What's the sensitivity surface look like? Can we get a heatmap by prompt?
[02:52] Diego: Yes — in the console 'Quality delta' heatmap shows per-prompt change vs baseline FP16. You can filter by length, intent, and error type.
[03:05] Samantha: Nice, can it show hallucination vs formatting errors?
[03:10] Diego: We tag common error classes via our eval harness, so yes — you'll see categories. It's not perfect automatic classification, sometimes it mistakes OOV names, there's noise.
[03:25] Ava: We do recommend a small human eval on samples flagged high-risk.
[03:32] [04:00] Diego: I'm switching to the demo sandbox run we did with a similar EHR workload — here's an INT8 profile applied, latency down ~40% on p95, cost down 30%, but the quality delta for clinical question answering is +2.7% wrong answers on our labeled set.
[04:20] Priya: That delta is concerning. How do you mitigate that?
[04:26] Diego: Two patterns: 1) hybrid profiles — INT8 for most layers but FP8 for attention or specific MLP layers; 2) fallback rules — if the confidence metric or QA score drops under threshold, route to FP8 or a higher-capacity model.
[04:45] Marcus: Does that routing add latency overhead?
[04:49] Diego: Minimal if you do prefix/kv-aware routing — initial attempt may be slightly slower for that single request, but overall SLOs stay intact because only a small percentage escalate.
[05:02] [05:30] Ava: Let me show the rule editor — you can create a rule: "if prompt contains 'acute' OR intent==diagnosis AND quality_score<0.92 -> route FP8/model-x". You can test it in-console with canned prompts.
[05:55] Priya: Can we run a canary on a subset of traffic? We prefer to pilot on 5% first.
[06:03] Diego: Absolutely, rollout is built-in. You can weight by route, by user segment, or by percentage. Logs show token-level diffs for failed cases.
[06:18] [06:50] Marcus: About the security stuff — for clinical logs, we need KMS and audit logs, does Optimize change anything?
[06:58] Ava: No differences. For Private/Dedicated customers we integrate with your KMS/HSM, and all audit logs are forwarded to your SIEM. We'll share the SOC2 + ISO docs after the call.
[07:12] Samantha: If we do Dedicated, do we still get Optimize console features?
[07:18] Diego: Yes — console + Optimize are available in Dedicated and Private; difference is control plane location and model artifact placement.
[07:30] [08:00] Diego: Back to profiles — here's an example of a hybrid profile we use in clinical pilots: INT8 on early transformer blocks, FP8 on last 3 layers + attention. That preserves final token distribution and reduces hallucination risk.
[08:20] Priya: How do you pick which layers to keep at higher precision?
[08:25] Diego: We run a layer-sensitivity sweep — incremental quantize of each layer and measure QA drop. The console visualizes per-layer sensitivity so you can craft an optimal profile without guessing.
[08:40] [09:05] Speaker 2 (background): yeah
[09:10] Diego: Also, if you have stricter latency P95 targets, we can bias profiles to speed — e.g., INT8 across the board and add a strict fallback for any low-confidence outputs.
[09:24] Samantha: Is there an ROI calculator?
[09:28] Ava: Yes, Optimize has a cost simulator. Enter your qprofile, traffic volume, token counts and it projects unit cost and monthly savings. We'll run this for Horizon's numbers in the POC.
[09:44] Priya: Walk me through what you need from us to start the POC.
[09:50] Ava: Three things: 1) 1-2k anonymized prompts representative of triage and summarization tasks, 2) baseline labels or success criteria (what constitutes a correct summary), and 3) decision on infrastructure (Hosted vs Dedicated pilot).
[10:04] Priya: We can share prompts, may take legal sign-off on data extraction. Marcus will help coordinate.
[10:12] Marcus: Yep, we'll produce an anonymized dump. Timeline: ~by July 12.
[10:18] Ava: Great. Diego — show the per-prompt diff for one sample?
[10:22] Diego: Sure — here's a sample patient-summarization prompt in the console test harness. Baseline FP16 answer and INT8 answer side-by-side. You can see difference: INT8 missed a negation in medication history. That's classic small-logit sensitivity.
[10:38] Priya: So we should prioritize negation-heavy prompts for human review.
[10:42] Diego: Exactly. And the console can tag those automatically if they contain certain patterns. It's probabilistic though — sometimes it flags benign variations.
[10:56] [11:30] Ava: We'll attach a short guide on how to assemble the sample set with de-identification suggestions.
[11:37] Samantha: For the POC run, can we test both INT8-first and FP8-first strategies and compare?
[11:44] Diego: Yep. The sandbox supports parallel runs and the Optimize report will give you per-strategy deltas, cost sims, and per-error-class breakdown.
[12:00] Priya: Curious on hardware sensitivity — if we move to our on-prem fleet later, will these qprofiles port?
[12:08] Diego: They should, but kernel availability varies. Our compilation layer selects optimized kernels — sometimes INT8 speedups are bigger on A100 vs newer accelerators, and FP8 runtime support is still catching up on some older infra. We'll note compatibility in the profile export.
[12:25] Marcus: So we'll want a compatibility matrix.
[12:28] Ava: We'll include that — we'll map profiles to known hardware families.
[12:33] [13:00] Diego: Another trick — confidence calibration. Even with INT8, we can calibrate the model's confidence estimator and use that as a cheap signal to trigger fallback instead of re-running expensive full-checks.
[13:17] Priya: What's the false positive rate on that approach?
[13:22] Diego: In our pilots it's ~3-6% extra fallbacks, but it prevents most critical misses. We'll show numbers from prior health pilots in the POC report.
[13:35] [14:00] Ava: Quick demo — creating a canary rule and pushing to 5% traffic. (typing) There, you can see traffic distribution and live logs.
[14:18] Samantha: The UI looks good, can we change JSON config for infra-as-code?
[14:24] Diego: Yes, every profile and rule has an API and CLI export, so you can check these into your infra repo.
[14:34] Marcus: How do we audit changes? Need CSOps audit trail for compliance.
[14:39] Ava: All changes are logged and tied to SSO identity; we also support webhooks to your audit pipeline.
[14:46] [15:10] Priya: Any risks we should be aware of?
[15:15] Diego: Main risk is underestimating distribution shift — if production prompts drift significantly from your sample set, profiles can break. So continuous monitoring with small human-in-the-loop checks at rollout is recommended.
[15:29] Samantha: How often do customers re-run sensitivity sweeps?
[15:33] Diego: Typically weekly during the POC, then monthly in steady-state or whenever you push a new prompt set or model version.
[15:41] [16:00] Ava: OK let's talk timelines: if you share prompts by July 12, Diego can run sweeps and have initial INT8/FP8 candidate profiles and a first report by July 15-16.
[16:12] Priya: That works. We'll also need pricing for monthly Dedicated vs Hosted Optimize add-on.
[16:20] Ava: I'll send pricing tiers today. Note Dedicated has a minimum commit but gives lower per-token costs for heavy throughput.
[16:31] Marcus: Are there any licensing constraints on model artifacts? If we use open models and quantize them, can we keep those artifacts on-prem?
[16:40] Diego: Yes, for Private/Dedicated you can host model artifacts in your VPC/on-prem. Our onboarding process includes an artifact transfer guide and license checklist.
[16:52] [17:30] Priya: How do you report regression alerts? We want automated alerts if a metric drops beyond a threshold.
[17:37] Diego: Optimize includes quality monitors and regression alerts tied to prompt sets; you define thresholds and alert targets (Slack, PagerDuty, email).
[17:49] Ava: We'll add a sample alerting policy for clinical severity levels.
[17:55] [19:00] Diego: Demo wrap-up — here's the export of the baseline run (CSV). It contains per-prompt FP16 vs INT8 diffs, QA flags, latency p50/p95. We'll attach this to the follow-up.
[19:18] Priya: Great. Any competitor differences we should consider?
[19:22] Ava: Compared to managed APIs like OpenAI, Redwood gives you more control over profiling and fine-grained fallback policies and better observability into token-level diffs. Versus Anthropic, feature parity depends on model family, but our focus is serving economics and safety controls.
[19:41] Marcus: Noted.
[19:46] [21:00] Samantha: Last question — do you support model surgery or layer remapping for stubborn cases?
[21:06] Diego: We don't alter model weights in the platform, but we support per-layer quantization overrides and some compensated kernels. For deeper surgery we'd work with you via Professional Services.
[21:21] Priya: OK, that's fine.
[21:26] Ava: Recap of next steps: Redwood to grant sandbox access, Priya/Marcus to share anonymized prompts by July 12, Diego to run sweeps and deliver an INT8/FP8 candidate report by July 15, Ava to send pricing + SOC2 docs by EOD tomorrow.
[21:48] Priya: Sounds good.
[21:51] Marcus: We'll confirm calendar for the follow-up.
[21:55] Ava: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the attachments.
[22:02] Diego: Thanks, bye.

(End of recording)

[04:26] Diego: Two patterns: 1) hybrid profiles — INT8 for most layers but FP8 for attention or specific MLP layers; 2) fallback rules — if the confidence metric or QA score drops under threshold, route to FP8 or a higher-capacity model.
[04:45] Marcus: Does that routing add latency overhead?
[04:49] Diego: Minimal if you do prefix/kv-aware routing — initial attempt may be slightly slower for that single request, but overall SLOs stay intact because only a small percentage escalate.
[05:02] [05:30] Ava: Let me show the rule editor — you can create a rule: "if prompt contains 'acute' OR intent==diagnosis AND quality_score<0.92 -> route FP8/model-x". You can test it in-console with canned prompts.
[05:55] Priya: Can we run a canary on a subset of traffic? We prefer to pilot on 5% first.
[06:03] Diego: Absolutely, rollout is built-in. You can weight by route, by user segment, or by percentage. Logs show token-level diffs for failed cases.
[06:18] [06:50] Marcus: About the security stuff — for clinical logs, we need KMS and audit logs, does Optimize change anything?
[06:58] Ava: No differences. For Private/Dedicated customers we integrate with your KMS/HSM, and all audit logs are forwarded to your SIEM. We'll share the SOC2 + ISO docs after the call.
[07:12] Samantha: If we do Dedicated, do we still get Optimize console features?
[07:18] Diego: Yes — console + Optimize are available in Dedicated and Private; difference is control plane location and model artifact placement.
[07:30] [08:00] Diego: Back to profiles — here's an example of a hybrid profile we use in clinical pilots: INT8 on early transformer blocks, FP8 on last 3 layers + attention. That preserves final token distribution and reduces hallucination risk.
[08:20] Priya: How do you pick which layers to keep at higher precision?
[08:25] Diego: We run a layer-sensitivity sweep — incremental quantize of each layer and measure QA drop. The console visualizes per-layer sensitivity so you can craft an optimal profile without guessing.
[08:40] [09:05] Speaker 2 (background): yeah
[09:10] Diego: Also, if you have stricter latency P95 targets, we can bias profiles to speed — e.g., INT8 across the board and add a strict fallback for any low-confidence outputs.
[09:24] Samantha: Is there an ROI calculator?
[09:28] Ava: Yes, Optimize has a cost simulator. Enter your qprofile, traffic volume, token counts and it projects unit cost and monthly savings. We'll run this for Horizon's numbers in the POC.
[09:44] Priya: Walk me through what you need from us to start the POC.
[09:50] Ava: Three things: 1) 1-2k anonymized prompts representative of triage and summarization tasks, 2) baseline labels or success criteria (what constitutes a correct summary), and 3) decision on infrastructure (Hosted vs Dedicated pilot).
[10:04] Priya: We can share prompts, may take legal sign-off on data extraction. Marcus will help coordinate.
[10:12] Marcus: Yep, we'll produce an anonymized dump. Timeline: ~by July 12.
[10:18] Ava: Great. Diego — show the per-prompt diff for one sample?
[10:22] Diego: Sure — here's a sample patient-summarization prompt in the console test harness. Baseline FP16 answer and INT8 answer side-by-side. You can see difference: INT8 missed a negation in medication history. That's classic small-logit sensitivity.
[10:38] Priya: So we should prioritize negation-heavy prompts for human review.
[10:42] Diego: Exactly. And the console can tag those automatically if they contain certain patterns. It's probabilistic though — sometimes it flags benign variations.
[10:56] [11:30] Ava: We'll attach a short guide on how to assemble the sample set with de-identification suggestions.
[11:37] Samantha: For the POC run, can we test both INT8-first and FP8-first strategies and compare?
[11:44] Diego: Yep. The sandbox supports parallel runs and the Optimize report will give you per-strategy deltas, cost sims, and per-error-class breakdown.
[12:00] Priya: Curious on hardware sensitivity — if we move to our on-prem fleet later, will these qprofiles port?
[12:08] Diego: They should, but kernel availability varies. Our compilation layer selects optimized kernels — sometimes INT8 speedups are bigger on A100 vs newer accelerators, and FP8 runtime support is still catching up on some older infra. We'll note compatibility in the profile export.
[12:25] Marcus: So we'll want a compatibility matrix.
[12:28] Ava: We'll include that — we'll map profiles to known hardware families.
[12:33] [13:00] Diego: Another trick — confidence calibration. Even with INT8, we can calibrate the model's confidence estimator and use that as a cheap signal to trigger fallback instead of re-running expensive full-checks.
[13:17] Priya: What's the false positive rate on that approach?
[13:22] Diego: In our pilots it's ~3-6% extra fallbacks, but it prevents most critical misses. We'll show numbers from prior health pilots in the POC report.
[13:35] [14:00] Ava: Quick demo — creating a canary rule and pushing to 5% traffic. (typing) There, you can see traffic distribution and live logs.
[14:18] Samantha: The UI looks good, can we change JSON config for infra-as-code?
[14:24] Diego: Yes, every profile and rule has an API and CLI export, so you can check these into your infra repo.
[14:34] Marcus: How do we audit changes? Need CSOps audit trail for compliance.
[14:39] Ava: All changes are logged and tied to SSO identity; we also support webhooks to your audit pipeline.
[14:46] [15:10] Priya: Any risks we should be aware of?
[15:15] Diego: Main risk is underestimating distribution shift — if production prompts drift significantly from your sample set, profiles can break. So continuous monitoring with small human-in-the-loop checks at rollout is recommended.
[15:29] Samantha: How often do customers re-run sensitivity sweeps?
[15:33] Diego: Typically weekly during the POC, then monthly in steady-state or whenever you push a new prompt set or model version.
[15:41] [16:00] Ava: OK let's talk timelines: if you share prompts by July 12, Diego can run sweeps and have initial INT8/FP8 candidate profiles and a first report by July 15-16.
[16:12] Priya: That works. We'll also need pricing for monthly Dedicated vs Hosted Optimize add-on.
[16:20] Ava: I'll send pricing tiers today. Note Dedicated has a minimum commit but gives lower per-token costs for heavy throughput.
[16:31] Marcus: Are there any licensing constraints on model artifacts? If we use open models and quantize them, can we keep those artifacts on-prem?
[16:40] Diego: Yes, for Private/Dedicated you can host model artifacts in your VPC/on-prem. Our onboarding process includes an artifact transfer guide and license checklist.
[16:52] [17:30] Priya: How do you report regression alerts? We want automated alerts if a metric drops beyond a threshold.
[17:37] Diego: Optimize includes quality monitors and regression alerts tied to prompt sets; you define thresholds and alert targets (Slack, PagerDuty, email).
[17:49] Ava: We'll add a sample alerting policy for clinical severity levels.
[17:55] [19:00] Diego: Demo wrap-up — here's the export of the baseline run (CSV). It contains per-prompt FP16 vs INT8 diffs, QA flags, latency p50/p95. We'll attach this to the follow-up.
[19:18] Priya: Great. Any competitor differences we should consider?
[19:22] Ava: Compared to managed APIs like OpenAI, Redwood gives you more control over profiling and fine-grained fallback policies and better observability into token-level diffs. Versus Anthropic, feature parity depends on model family, but our focus is serving economics and safety controls.
[19:41] Marcus: Noted.
[19:46] [21:00] Samantha: Last question — do you support model surgery or layer remapping for stubborn cases?
[21:06] Diego: We don't alter model weights in the platform, but we support per-layer quantization overrides and some compensated kernels. For deeper surgery we'd work with you via Professional Services.
[21:21] Priya: OK, that's fine.
[21:26] Ava: Recap of next steps: Redwood to grant sandbox access, Priya/Marcus to share anonymized prompts by July 12, Diego to run sweeps and deliver an INT8/FP8 candidate report by July 15, Ava to send pricing + SOC2 docs by EOD tomorrow.
[21:48] Priya: Sounds good.
[21:51] Marcus: We'll confirm calendar for the follow-up.
[21:55] Ava: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the attachments.
[22:02] Diego: Thanks, bye.

(End of recording)

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-03-21
Time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake, Priya Singh, Luis Marten, Ava Romero (Redwood); Alex Navarro, Mei Chen, Sam Ortiz, Rina Patel (Horizon Health)

Summary:
Quick sync to close out the postmortem actions from last week's outage, confirm safety-net/oncall handover for the pilot, review outstanding security/compliance blockers, and plan a low-risk benchmark in Horizon staging. Tone: pragmatic, a little tense but cooperative.

Topics:
- Root-cause recap and lingering unknowns
- Safety-net (fallbacks + graceful degrade) responsibilities
- Data residency and KMS questions from compliance
- Staging benchmark plan + KPIs
- Next steps and owners

Action items (brief): see metadata list above

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. We wanted to keep this tight — half hour-ish, plus followups. I'm Jordan, account lead. Priya and Luis from our side. Alex, Mei — thanks for making time.

[00:18] Alex Navarro: Yeah, thanks. We had a rough week so appreciate the off hours help. We're mostly looking to make sure the oncall handoff is clear and the mitigation you suggested is actually codified.

[00:32] Priya Singh: Hi all. I'll start with the quick recap — from Redwood's perspective, the primary failure mode was a KV cache eviction storm combined with an autoscale thrash during a traffic spike; some requests fell back to a heavier model path that wasn't warmed, which increased latency and caused retries. There were also a couple of LT-establishing network blips between zones.

[00:58] Mei Chen: Right, and from our logs we saw the tail latency blow up around 03:14 UTC; there's a pattern where a prefix cache miss cascades because many of our prompts share the same cold prefix — so the cache churn was worse than we'd modeled.

[01:22] Jordan Blake: That's helpful. I want to be explicit about responsibilities for the safety-net flow: Redwood will maintain the fallback policy and the canary route definitions; Horizon needs to set the priority on model variants and confirm which prompts are allowed to use the cached prefix optimization.

[01:40] Sam Ortiz: Quick clarifying question — when you say "fallback policy", is that run-time within Redwood Dedicated, or do we need to add client-side checks that preemptively choose a lighter model? We tried a client-side guard but it complicated metrics.

[02:01] Priya Singh: Good question. We recommend server-side routing, primarily because it preserves a single source of truth for capacity and fallback. Client-side guards are an option but they make tracing harder. For the pilot we'll do server-side policy and we'll expose a header so you can observe decisions in your traces.

[02:20] Rina Patel: On compliance, can you confirm that the fallback logs won't include raw prompt text? We need to be careful about PII leaving our VPC — last time some audit tooling caught free text in a debug trace.

[02:39] Ava Romero: Short answer: we can mask or hash prompt strings in logs; by default debug traces include snippets but for enterprise pilots we flip a mask-on policy. We'll include an audit example in the security pack.

[02:55] Alex Navarro: Excellent. Also, we need to talk SLA-wise — if tail latency breaches we want a documented escalation path. Who's first responder on Redwood's side?

[03:10] Luis Marten: For the pilot we'll provide a temporary oncall rotation: Luis and Ava will be primary weekday coverage; Priya will be on-call for deep infra escalations. We'll share a dedicated Slack channel and an incident bridging doc.

[03:27] Mei Chen: Can we get the oncall roster in writing? We need pager policy for our team to match.

[03:35] Jordan Blake: Yep, action item for Luis to share the rota.

[03:42] Sam Ortiz: Two technical items: 1) we want to test the prefix cache tuning you recommended (larger cache, different eviction), and 2) quantify cost impact if we stick to the warmed model path vs allow fallback — our finance team is worried.

[04:02] Priya Singh: For (1) we'll provide a param set — cache size, TTL, batching window — and a test harness to run in staging. For (2) we can run a cost delta using your traffic profile; we did a rough sim in the trial doc but we'll do a more conservative estimate with your exact sequence lengths.

[04:28] Alex Navarro: There's also one lingering unknown from the postmortem: the cross-zone network jitter. Was that AWS, or was it something in the customer routing?

[04:41] Priya Singh: Our tracing indicates packet retransmits at the edge managed by your cloud provider; we saw higher SRTT in one AZ. We're not ruling out provider-level transient issues. We'll include the packet-level logs in the handover doc.

[04:58] Mei Chen: Okay. If it's provider, we'll raise with our cloud TAM. But we still want Redwood to handle degraded performance gracefully.

[05:11] Jordan Blake: Absolutely. Let's run through a concrete safety-net play:
- Step A: Redwood notices rising tail latency and triggers fallback routing to a smaller model variant in the same family.
- Step B: Redwood reduces batch windows and disables speculative prefetch for cold prefixes.
- Step C: If latency still high, Redwood returns a short-circuit response with a 10 token summary and a warning header; Horizon can choose to requeue for async processing.

[05:45] Sam Ortiz: That short-circuit behavior — is that implemented now or is this an as-of-pilot change?

[05:53] Priya Singh: We have a proto that does short-circuits; for the pilot we'll enable it behind a feature flag and provide telemetry so you can see how often it fires.

[06:05] Rina Patel: We need the short-circuit not to expose any PII; treat it like a synthesized summary, not an excerpt.

[06:12] Ava Romero: Confirmed, summarize-only policy when short-circuiting.

[06:20] Alex Navarro: Let's talk about the security blockers — KMS integration, SSO access for our ops, and data residency. Rina's team is watching the KMS keys very closely.

[06:33] Rina Patel: We require customer-managed keys (CMKs) for any model logs that persist outside the VPC. Does Redwood support bring-your-own-key (BYOK)?

[06:42] Priya Singh: Yes, we support KMS integration and HSM for Private deployments. For the hosted pilot we can accept customer-managed keys for storage encryption and for audit logs; if you need HSM-backed signing we can discuss Private options.

[06:58] Mei Chen: Will the key operations add latency to inference? Our compliance team is concerned about chattiness to KMS for every request.

[07:07] Priya Singh: We don't call KMS per inference token — keys are used for at-rest encryption and for signing audit artifacts. For per-request auth we use short-lived service tokens, so runtime impact is minimal. We'll include an architecture diagram in the security pack to show call flow.

[07:23] Jordan Blake: On data residency, Horizon required that logs from the pilot remain in EU region — I think your main cluster is in us-west, correct?

[07:33] Alex Navarro: Correct, primary is us-west but we need EU for PHI concern.

[07:39] Priya Singh: For the pilot we can provision a regional endpoint in eu-west-1. It'll be a small dedicated pool; there will be a modest add-on for regional isolation. We'll outline costs in the pricing doc.

[07:52] Sam Ortiz: Good. Also want to lock down audit retention — how long will you keep obfuscated logs vs raw debug?

[07:59] Ava Romero: By default we keep obfuscated audit logs for 90 days; raw debug is disabled for enterprise pilots or retained only with explicit opt-in for a short period. We'll make retention configurable.

[08:14] Mei Chen: Moving onto the benchmark — can you run a staging job that simulates our peak burst: 18k rps over 3 minutes with mixed sequence lengths (2k short, 30% medium, 5% long)?

[08:28] Priya Singh: Yes. We'll use Redwood Dedicated parity to emulate capacity: set up the same batch windows and cache settings. We may need a small harness agent deployed in your VPC to replay traffic without sending PHI to Redwood's control plane.

[08:45] Alex Navarro: We'd prefer a replay agent to reduce data leaving our environment. Is that supported?

[08:50] Priya Singh: Yep, we have a replay agent (sometimes auto-called 'firewall-agent' in previous docs — transcription note: might be 'fairwall') that runs in customer VPC, signs requests with short-lived creds and only sends tokens to the Redwood endpoint. We'll include deployment steps.

[09:06] Sam Ortiz: If we run the replay agent, will Redwood still see prompt contents for caching decisions? We need to ensure the cache keys are hashed and not stored raw.

[09:18] Priya Singh: Cache keys are hashed and salted at the edge; we never store raw prompt text in the global cache snapshot that's visible to Redwood multitenant layers. For a dedicated/private pool, keys remain in your dedicated cache instances.

[09:35] Mei Chen: There's one more earlier item: last postmortem called out a runaway retry loop from our client SDK when it saw a 503; that created extra pressure. Have you got a recommended client-side backoff?

[09:48] Priya Singh: We recommend exponential backoff with jitter and a max retry budget per token. Also set client-side max inflight to avoid compounding. We'll share suggested defaults for our SDKs.

[10:02] Alex Navarro: Can you put those defaults in writing — our SDK team wants exact numbers.

[10:06] Priya Singh: Yep, action item.

[10:12] [Brief silence and note about screen share where Priya opened the runbook]

[10:22] Priya Singh: I'm going to walk through the runbook bits we updated: detect-level thresholds, automatic fallbacks, alert routing, and runbook links. The thresholds are adaptive by default but for the pilot we'll set fixed SLO thresholds you can change.

[10:40] Jordan Blake: Do you have a sample alert play? We want to ensure our oncall doesn't escalate too early.

[10:47] Priya Singh: Yes — three-tier alerts: 1) Warning (5m average tail > 500ms), 2) Critical (5m tail > 1.5s or error rate > 2%), 3) Outage (service unavailable > 2m). Warning goes to Slack ops channel, Critical pages Redwood oncall and Horizon primary, Outage triggers bridge.

[11:10] Mei Chen: That seems sensible. How about throttling? Can Redwood throttle downstream consumers gracefully?

[11:17] Priya Singh: We can apply per-customer rate-limits and also prioritize internal critical routes. For client throttling, we return 429 with a Retry-After; our SDKs handle backoff automatically if used.

[11:31] Alex Navarro: Competitor callout — we evaluated Megacloud AI earlier; they offered a cheaper failover but didn't have a private key option. NimbusServe had a more complex oncall integration. We're trying to pick a partner that can do the hybrid fit.

[11:47] Jordan Blake: Understood. We'll make sure to highlight BYOK and regional pool for EU in the proposal.

[11:55] Sam Ortiz: One more operational ask — can Redwood provide a weekly 'health snapshot' for the pilot that includes cache hit ratios, tail latency P95/P99, error budget burn and short-circuit counts?

[12:08] Luis Marten: Yes, the console already has dashboards and we can provide an export to your SIEM. We'll include a sample CSV and also a dashboard share link.

[12:20] Mei Chen: Export to our SIEM is mandatory — we ingest into Splunk and run custom alerts there.

[12:27] Ava Romero: We'll confirm the log schema.

[12:34] [Noise/background: someone typing; mild crosstalk]

[12:45] Jordan Blake: Alright let's walkthrough the timeline we proposed for the next two weeks: security pack by 24th, traffic/profile by 25th, pricing and commitment by 26th, staging benchmark week of 30th, and pilot kickoff the week after if all good.

[13:05] Alex Navarro: That timeline works but we need to ensure procurement reviews the pricing by 26th — if we don't have that, the pilot won't start.

[13:12] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize it.

[13:20] Rina Patel: On procurement, any standard contract terms that are blockers? We need data processing addendum (DPA) and breach notification timelines.

[13:30] Jordan Blake: We have a standard DPA and commit to 72-hour breach notification for severity-one. We'll include DPA and an escrow clause for the pilot.

[13:42] Sam Ortiz: Back to the technicals — during the outage we noticed a memory spike in the inference worker that correlated with the long-sequence jobs; have you implemented a soft-limit that will reject very long sequences to protect the pool?

[13:58] Priya Singh: We do. There's a per-tenant maximum token limit and an optional queue for large jobs. For the pilot we recommend allowing long jobs but routing them to a separate gradual pool to avoid interfering with low-latency traffic.

[14:14] Mei Chen: So separate pool for L long sequences. Is there autoscale there?

[14:19] Priya Singh: Yes, separate autoscale group with different burst parameters. We'll size it conservatively for the pilot.

[14:28] Alex Navarro: Cost question — if we route 5% long sequences to the separate pool, what's rough incremental cost?

[14:34] Priya Singh: We'll model that with your exact sequence length distribution. Rough guess: 12-18% uplift depending on quantization and batch efficiency. We'll run a sim and include it in the pricing doc.

[14:47] [Longer pause while people read shared doc; Jordan prompts next topic]

[14:58] Jordan Blake: Anything else on the postmortem items we haven't closed? I know we had a doc item about a telemetry gap — who owns closing that?

[15:06] Mei Chen: That was the telemetry agent crash for 30 minutes; we need a fix and a followup test to ensure metrics don't drop during restarts.

[15:13] Priya Singh: Priya: we'll add a heartbeat to the agent and replay missed metrics into the pipeline; we'll test it in staging.

[15:22] Alex Navarro: Good. Also, the PM asked that we get an RCA note that is customer-friendly — no raw logs, just timeline and actions. Can Redwood produce that?

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll produce a customer-ready RCA and link to the raw artifacts behind a signed access for your security team.

[15:40] Rina Patel: And confirm: will the raw artifacts require a signed NDA or special access?

[15:47] Jordan Blake: We'll gate raw debug artifacts behind a short NDA and restrict to named engineers.

[15:54] [Short crosstalk; Alex affirms]

[16:02] Priya Singh: I want to call out one engineering caveat — the short-circuit summary can change model quality; we recommend adding a small human-in-the-loop step for high sensitivity calls where accuracy matters. For non-critical UX, auto short-circuiting is fine.

[16:22] Sam Ortiz: We'll flag high sensitivity endpoints in the request header so you can skip short-circuit.

[16:30] Priya Singh: Perfect.

[16:35] Jordan Blake: Let's document the acceptance criteria for pilot success — suggests: sustained 99th percentile latency below 1.2s for first wave, cache hit ratio > 85% for identified prefixes, and no unrecoverable outages in 2 weeks. Thoughts?

[16:52] Mei Chen: That feels reasonable. Maybe tweak P99 to 1.5s for the first week to be realistic.

[17:01] Alex Navarro: Agree to 1.5s first week, then ramp to 1.2s after tuning.

[17:09] Jordan Blake: Noted.

[17:12] [Participants make small jokes; lighter tone]

[17:25] Luis Marten: On the ops side, we'll schedule a 30-minute dry run with your oncall to walk through the runbook triggers before the benchmark. Suggested time: 2026-03-29.

[17:37] Mei Chen: That works. Put it on calendar.

[17:42] Jordan Blake: Final round — any blocking items that would stop the pilot?

[17:48] Rina Patel: The main blocker is DPA signoff and BYOK confirmation. If we get those done by 24th, we're green.

[17:56] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize DPA + BYOK.

[18:03] Alex Navarro: One administrative question: will Redwood accept SOC2 evidence as-of last audit or do you need live attestations?

[18:12] Ava Romero: We can provide the latest SOC2 report and our compliance contact for any attestation questions. For some customers we also offer a SOC2+ supplemental letter.

[18:25] Jordan Blake: Good. 

[18:30] [Wrap-up begins]

[18:32] Jordan Blake: Recap — Redwood to share security pack (SOC2, ISO, KMS diagrams), Priya to send benchmark runbook and backoff defaults, Luis to share oncall rota, Horizon to share traffic/profile and PII prompt examples. We'll schedule the dry run 3/29 and tentatively run the staging benchmark week of 3/30. Pricing proposal to Alex by 3/26. Anything to add?

[18:58] Mei Chen: No, that's it. Thank you.

[19:02] Alex Navarro: Thanks — appreciate the concrete plan.

[19:06] Jordan Blake: Thanks everyone. We'll send notes and action items after this.

[19:12] [Meeting ends, approximate total duration 54 minutes — transcript contains occasional misrecognitions: 'firewall-agent' transcribed as 'fairwall' in one excerpt, product 'Redwood Dedicated' sometimes appears as 'Redwood Dedicate' in auto text)
